structure.title: Preface
structure.summary:
This document is the official technical specification for the Universal Chiplet Interconnect Express (UCIe), Revision 1.1, Version 1.0, dated July 10, 2023. It serves as a comprehensive standard for die-to-die (D2D) interconnects, designed to enable high-performance communication between chiplets within a single package.

The main points covered in the document include:

*   **Architectural Layers:** Detailed specifications for the UCIe stack, including the **Protocol Layer** (supporting PCIe, CXL, and Streaming protocols), the **Die-to-Die (D2D) Adapter**, the **Logical Physical Layer**, and the **Electrical Layer**.
*   **Configurations and Packaging:** Definitions for various implementation scenarios, specifically distinguishing between **Advanced Package** (optimized for performance/short reach) and **Standard Package** interfaces, as well as single-module and multi-module configurations.
*   **Link Management and Initialization:** Extensive procedures for link initialization, training (including Data-to-Clock training), state machine hierarchies, and power management link states.
*   **Reliability and Error Handling:** Mechanisms for maintaining data integrity, including Cyclic Redundancy Check (CRC) computation, retry rules, runtime link testing using parity, and interconnect redundancy remapping (lane repair).
*   **Electrical and Physical Specifications:** Detailed requirements for transmitters, receivers, clocking architectures, supply noise, and channel specifications (including bump maps for different package types).
*   **Sideband Communication:** A dedicated specification for the sideband protocol, covering packet types, formats, and flow control for management and control tasks.
*   **Software and Configuration:** Guidance on software discovery of UCIe links, register locations, and detailed descriptions of various UCIe registers (such as DVSEC and D2D/PHY register blocks) for system management and error logging.
*   **Interface Definitions:** Technical definitions for the Raw Die-to-Die Interface (RDI) and the Flit-Aware Die-to-Die Interface (FDI).
*   **Compliance and Interoperability:** Standards for testing compliance across the protocol, adapter, and physical layers, as well as notes on interoperability with other technologies like AIB.

structure.title: Terminology
structure.summary:
This partial document is the introductory section of the Universal Chiplet Interconnect express (UCIe) Specification, Revision 1.1, Version 1.0, dated July 10, 2023. The main points covered include:

*   **Terminology and Definitions:** A comprehensive, multi-page glossary defining technical terms, acronyms, and abbreviations used throughout the specification. This includes definitions for hardware components (e.g., PHY, Sideband, Mainband, Modules), signaling concepts (e.g., Lane, Flit, Unit Interval), packaging technologies (e.g., Standard Package, Advanced Package, CoWoS), and protocol-specific terms related to PCIe and CXL.
*   **Reference Documents:** A list of external technical specifications that serve as foundational references for the UCIe standard, specifically citing the PCI Express (PCIe) Base Specification (Revision 6.0.1), the Compute Express Link (CXL) Specification (Revision 3.1), and the ACPI Specification (version 6.5 or later).

structure.title: Reference Documents
structure.summary:
This partial document is an excerpt from the Universal Chiplet Interconnect express (UCIe) Specification, Revision 1.1, Version 1.0, dated July 10, 2023. It primarily contains front-matter administrative and foundational information, including:

*   **Terms and Definitions:** A glossary of technical acronyms and terms used throughout the specification, such as UCIe Raw Format, Unit Interval, Upstream Port, and various register blocks.
*   **Reference Documents:** A list of external industry standards that the UCIe specification relies upon, specifically citing PCI Express (Revision 6.0.1), Compute Express Link (Revision 3.1), and ACPI (version 6.5 or later).
*   **Revision History:** A summary of changes between version 1.0 and 1.1, highlighting key updates such as Streaming Flit Format capabilities, enhanced multi-protocol multiplexing, support for x32 Advanced Package Modules, Link Health Monitoring, and various hardware capability definitions and bug fixes.

structure.title: Revision History
structure.summary:
This document is an excerpt from the Universal Chiplet Interconnect express (UCIe) Specification, specifically covering the revision history and the introductory overview of the architecture. 

The main points covered include:

*   **Revision History:** A summary of the updates in Revision 1.1 (released July 10, 2023) compared to the initial 1.0 release. Key enhancements in version 1.1 include streaming flit format capabilities, enhanced multi-protocol multiplexing, support for x32 advanced package modules, link health monitoring, and hardware compliance definitions.
*   **UCIe Architecture Overview:** An introduction to UCIe as an open, multi-protocol, on-package interconnect standard designed to support disaggregated die architectures. It aims to enable interoperability across diverse devices by providing a common physical and link layer.
*   **Protocol Support:** Details on the protocols supported by UCIe, including PCIe, CXL, Streaming, and a protocol-agnostic "Raw Format." The document explains how mapping these protocols via Flit Formats can improve power and performance compared to traditional SERDES PHYs.
*   **System Integration and Use Cases:** An explanation of how UCIe facilitates SoC construction by encompassing application layers and package form-factors (such as bump location and power delivery). It provides illustrative examples of how CPU dies, accelerator dies, and I/O tiles can be interconnected using various protocols to manage coherency, memory, and external connectivity.

structure.title: Introduction
structure.summary:
This document is an introductory chapter from the Universal Chiplet Interconnect express (UCIe) Specification, Revision 1.1. It provides a high-level overview of the UCIe architecture, which is an open, multi-protocol, on-package interconnect standard designed to facilitate a disaggregated die ecosystem.

The main points covered in the document include:

*   **Core Purpose and Protocol Support:** UCIe serves as a common physical and link layer for connecting multiple dies on the same package. It supports multiple protocols, including PCIe, CXL, and Streaming, as well as a protocol-agnostic "Raw Format" for custom mappings.
*   **Architecture and Interoperability:** The specification encompasses the application layer and package-level elements (such as bump location and power delivery) to ensure interoperability across diverse devices. It includes mechanisms for debugging and compliance.
*   **Usage Models and Connectivity:** 
    *   **On-Package:** Demonstrates how CPU dies, accelerators, and I/O tiles can be interconnected using various protocols (e.g., CXL for coherency).
    *   **Off-Package:** Explains how UCIe Retimers can extend connectivity beyond the package to a rack or pod level using electrical or optical cables.
*   **Packaging Options:** The document distinguishes between two primary packaging technologies (noting that 3D packaging is not covered in this version):
    *   **Standard Package (2D):** A low-cost, long-reach option (10mm to 25mm) using organic substrates.
    *   **Advanced Package (2.5D):** A performance-optimized option for high bandwidth and low latency, featuring short reach (less than 2mm) and technologies like silicon bridges or interposers.
*   **Layered Protocol Structure:** It introduces the UCIe stack as a layered protocol, noting that the architecture is divided into distinct functional layers (Application, Protocol, and Physical/Link layers).

structure.title: UCIe Components
structure.summary:
This partial document is an excerpt from the Universal Chiplet Interconnect express (UCIe) Specification (Revision 1.1). It outlines the fundamental architecture and technical characteristics of the UCIe standard, specifically focusing on the following main points:

*   **Advanced Package Technology:** The document describes the "Advanced Package" option, which is optimized for high bandwidth, low latency, and power efficiency. It specifies technical parameters such as supported speeds (ranging from 4 GT/s to 32 GT/s), bump pitch (25 um to 55 um), a short channel reach (under 2 mm), and Raw Bit Error Rate (BER) requirements. It also references various physical implementation examples like Silicon Bridges (e.g., EMIB) and Interposers (e.g., CoWoS).
*   **UCIe Layered Architecture:** The specification defines UCIe as a layered protocol consisting of three main components: the Protocol Layer, the Die-to-Die (D2D) Adapter, and the Physical Layer. It notes that each component must support the advertised functionality and bandwidth.
*   **Protocol Layer:** This layer handles application-specific transfers. The document identifies supported protocols, including PCIe, CXL (with specific exclusions), and a generic Streaming Protocol for user-defined data.
*   **Die-to-Die (D2D) Adapter:** This component acts as the intermediary between the Protocol and Physical layers. Its responsibilities include coordinating data transfer, managing link states, performing parameter negotiation, and handling error correction (CRC and Retry schemes) when necessary. It also manages ARB/MUX functionality for simultaneous protocols and power management coordination.

nodes.title: Protocol Layer
nodes.summary:
This partial document provides an overview of the Universal Chiplet Interconnect Express (UCIe) Specification, specifically focusing on the architecture of its layers and functionalities. The main points covered include:

*   **Protocol Layer:** Details the supported protocols for data transfer, including PCIe, CXL (with specific exclusions like RCD/RCH), and a generic Streaming Protocol for user-defined protocols. It notes that different optimizations and Flit formats are available for each.
*   **Die-to-Die (D2D) Adapter:** Describes the role of the D2D Adapter in coordinating between the Protocol and Physical layers to ensure low-latency data transfer. Key responsibilities include managing ARB/MUX functionality for multiple protocols, implementing CRC and Retry schemes for error correction, managing link state machines, parameter negotiation, and coordinating power management and link training.
*   **Layered Architecture Overview:** Through text and a functional diagram, the document outlines the relationship between the Protocol Layer, the D2D Adapter (including its sub-functions like Link State Management and CRC/Retry), and the Physical Layer (including Electrical/AFE and Sideband components).

nodes.title: Die-to-Die (D2D) Adapter
nodes.summary:
This partial document provides a technical overview of the architecture and layers of the Universal Chiplet Interconnect express (UCIe) Specification, Revision 1.1. It details the functional hierarchy of the UCIe stack, specifically focusing on the following four components:

* **Protocol Layer:** Describes the supported protocols for data transfer, including PCIe, CXL (with specific exclusions), and a generic Streaming Protocol for user-defined applications. It notes that different optimizations and Flit formats are available for each.
* **Die-to-Die (D2D) Adapter:** Explains the adapter's role in coordinating data transfer between the Protocol and Physical layers. Key responsibilities include minimizing latency, managing ARB/MUX functionality for multiple protocols, implementing CRC and Retry schemes for error correction, and handling link state management, parameter negotiation, and power management.
* **Physical Layer:** Outlines the structural organization of the physical connection, which is divided into "Modules" of lanes. It distinguishes between two types of connections:
    * **Sideband:** An "always on" connection used for parameter exchanges, debugging, and link training, featuring a fixed 800MHz clock.
    * **Mainband:** The primary data path, which varies in lane count (N) depending on whether a Standard Package (x16) or Advanced Package (x32 or x64) is used.
* **Interfaces:** Identifies the two primary interfaces used to connect the layers: the Raw D2D Interface (RDI) between the Physical Layer and the D2D Adapter, and the Flit-aware D2D Interface (FDI) between the D2D Adapter and the Protocol Layer.

nodes.title: Physical Layer
nodes.summary:
This partial document provides a technical overview of the Physical Layer and Interfaces within the Universal Chiplet Interconnect Express (UCIe) Specification, Revision 1.1. 

The main points covered include:
* **Physical Layer Components:** An explanation of the UCIe data path organization, which uses "Modules" (groups of Lanes) as the fundamental unit for structural design and bandwidth scaling.
* **Connection Types:** A detailed breakdown of the two primary connection types:
    * **Sideband:** Used for parameter exchange, debugging, and link management; it operates on an "always on" auxiliary power domain with a fixed 800MHz clock.
    * **Mainband:** The primary data path consisting of a forwarded clock, data valid pin, track pin, and N Lanes of data.
* **Packaging Options:** A comparison between "Standard Package" (x16 lanes, no repair pins) and "Advanced Package" (x32 or x64 lanes, including redundant pins for lane repair).
* **Logical Physical Layer:** The role of the logical layer in coordinating link bring-up, training, and management functions.
* **Interfaces:** An introduction to the defined interfaces between the Physical Layer and the D2D Adapter (Raw D2D Interface) and between the D2D Adapter and the Protocol Layer (Flit-aware D2D Interface).

nodes.title: Interfaces
nodes.summary:
This partial document is an excerpt from the Universal Chiplet Interconnect Express (UCIe) Specification (Revision 1.1), specifically focusing on the Physical Layer and its interfaces. The main points covered include:

*   **Physical Layer Components:** The document defines the Physical Layer as being composed of three sub-components and organized into "Modules," which serve as the atomic unit for structural design.
*   **Connection Types:** It distinguishes between two types of connections within the physical link:
    *   **Sideband:** Used for parameter exchange, debug, and link management; it operates on an "always on" auxiliary power domain with a fixed 800MHz clock.
    *   **Mainband:** The primary data path consisting of a forwarded clock, data valid pin, track pin, and N lanes of data.
*   **Package Options:** The text details the differences between **Advanced Packages** (supporting x64 or x32 lane configurations with redundant pins for repair) and **Standard Packages** (supporting x16 lane configurations without repair pins).
*   **Interfaces:** It describes the two primary interfaces used to facilitate interoperability between vendors: the **Raw D2D Interface (RDI)** between the Physical Layer and the D2D Adapter, and the **Flit-aware D2D Interface** between the D2D Adapter and the Protocol Layer. The goal of these interfaces is to reduce integration costs and enable easier IP mixing and matching.
*   **UCIe Configurations:** The document introduces the concept of "Single Module" configurations for both Advanced and Standard packages, noting that multiple modules can operate independently.

structure.title: UCIe Configurations
structure.summary: This partial document is an excerpt from the Universal Chiplet Interconnect Express (UCIe) Specification, Revision 1.1. It outlines the primary objectives of the UCIe standard, emphasizing the ability for vendors and SoC builders to mix and match different IP layers (Protocol, Die-to-Die Adapter, and Physical Layer) from various providers to reduce integration costs and accelerate time to market. It also highlights the importance of consistent Bus Functional Models (BFMs) to mitigate the high costs of post-silicon interoperability testing. Additionally, the document introduces UCIe configurations, specifically detailing the "Single Module configuration" for both Advanced Packages (utilizing x64 or x32 data interfaces) and Standard Packages (utilizing x16 data interfaces), noting that multiple independent instantiations can operate at varying data rates and widths.

nodes.title: Single Module configuration
nodes.summary:
This partial document is an excerpt from the Universal Chiplet Interconnect Express (UCIe) Specification, Revision 1.1. It outlines the fundamental objectives and technical configurations of the UCIe standard. The main points covered include:

* **Core Objectives:** The specification aims to enable vendors and SoC builders to mix and match different IP layers (Protocol, Adapter, and Physical) from various providers to reduce integration costs and accelerate time-to-market. It also emphasizes the importance of consistent Bus Functional Models (BFMs) to streamline IP development and reduce post-silicon testing overhead.
* **UCIe Configurations:** The text details different operational permutations, specifically:
    * **Single Module Configuration:** Describes x64 or x32 data interfaces in Advanced Packages and x16 interfaces in Standard Packages, noting that multiple independent modules can operate at different rates and widths.
    * **Multi-module Configurations:** Covers two-module and four-module setups, specifying that when sharing a common Adapter, these modules must operate at the same data rate and width.
* **UCIe Retimers:** The document explains the role of Retimers in enabling "Off Package Interconnects." Retimers are used to extend the channel reach between UCIe dies located on different packages by providing local UCIe Link connections and external long-reach connections.

nodes.title: Multi-module Configurations
nodes.summary:
This partial document is an excerpt from the Universal Chiplet Interconnect Express (UCIe) Specification, Revision 1.1. It covers two primary technical areas:

* **Multi-module Configurations:** It describes the support for two-module and four-module configurations. It specifies that when these modules share a common Adapter, they must operate using the same data rate and width. The text references various figures illustrating these configurations for both Standard and Advanced Packages.
* **UCIe Retimers:** It explains the role of UCIe Retimers in enabling Off-Package Interconnects to extend the communication reach between UCIe dies located on different packages. It details how a Retimer maintains a local UCIe Link connection to an on-package die while providing an external connection for longer-distance transmission.

structure.title: UCIe Retimers
structure.summary:
This partial document is an excerpt from the Universal Chiplet Interconnect express (UCIe) Specification, Revision 1.1. It covers three primary technical areas:

* **Multi-module Configurations:** It outlines the support for two-module and four-module configurations, noting that when using a common Adapter, all modules must operate at the same data rate and width. It distinguishes between configurations for Standard and Advanced Packages.

* **UCIe Retimers:** The document details the use of Retimers to extend channel reach for Off-Package Interconnects between UCIe Dies on different packages. Key responsibilities of a Retimer include:
    * **Reliable Flit Transfer:** Providing three different methods for error handling (using native protocol FEC/CRC, providing independent FEC/CRC/Retry, or combining native CRC with custom FEC).
    * **Parameter Resolution:** Ensuring end-to-end interoperability by resolving link width, speed, and protocol parameters with a "remote Retimer partner," including the use of a "Stall" response to prevent timeouts.
    * **Link State Resolution:** Managing Link State Machine (LSM) or RDI states for end-to-end operation.
    * **Flow Control and Back-pressure:** Implementing credit-based flow control for data moving from a UCIe Die to a Retimer (where one credit equals 256B), while noting that data moving from a Retimer to a Die is not flow-controlled at the D2D adapter level.

* **Key Performance Targets:** The document provides a summary table of performance metrics for both Advanced and Standard Packages. These metrics include Link Speed/Voltage, Die Edge Bandwidth Density (based on bump pitch), Energy Efficiency (measured in pJ/bit), and Latency Targets (specifically targeting $\le$2ns at 16 GT/s).

structure.title: UCIe Key Performance Targets
structure.summary:
This partial document is an excerpt from the Universal Chiplet Interconnect express (UCIe) Specification, Revision 1.1. It outlines technical requirements and performance benchmarks for chiplet interconnects, focusing on three primary areas:

* **Flow Control and Back-pressure:** It details the credit-based flow control mechanism used for data transmitted from a UCIe Die to a UCIe Retimer. This includes requirements for Receiver Buffers, credit advertisement during parameter exchanges, the definition of a credit (256B), and protocols for handling state transitions and buffer management. It also notes that data from a Retimer to a Die is not flow-controlled at the D2D adapter level.

* **Key Performance Targets:** The document provides a summary of performance metrics across different link speeds and packaging types (Advanced vs. Standard). Key metrics include Die Edge Bandwidth Density, Energy Efficiency (measured in pJ/bit), Supply Voltage, and Latency targets (specifically targeting $\le$2ns at 16 GT/s).

* **Interoperability:** It discusses the necessity for package designers to ensure compatibility across interconnects, protocols, and voltage levels. It provides recommendations for transmitter voltages to ensure future-proofing and categorizes different bump pitches into four distinct groups (ranging from 25 $\mu$m to 55 $\mu$m) with corresponding minimum and expected maximum frequency ranges to guarantee interoperability.

structure.title: Interoperability
structure.summary:
This partial document from the Universal Chiplet Interconnect express (UCIe) Specification (Revision 1.1) outlines key technical requirements for interoperability and the architecture of the Protocol Layer. 

The main points covered include:

*   **Interoperability Requirements:** It emphasizes the need for compatibility across package interconnects, protocols, and voltage levels. It specifically recommends a transmitter voltage of less than 0.85 V to ensure compatibility across various process nodes.
*   **Bump Pitch and Frequency:** The document details how interoperability is managed across different bump pitch groups (ranging from 25 $\mu$m to 55 $\mu$m). It notes that while higher frequencies (up to 32 GT/s) are possible, designs may reduce maximum advertised frequencies in smaller bump pitch scenarios to manage power, thermal, and area constraints.
*   **Protocol Layer Architecture:** The specification explains that UCIe maps PCIe, CXL, and generic Streaming Protocols. It highlights the separation of protocol-related features from Flit formats and packetization, noting that different transport mechanisms can be used for different protocols (e.g., using CXL.io 68B Flit Format to transport PCIe non-Flit mode packets).
*   **Supported Protocol Mappings:** It lists the specific supported modes, including PCIe Flit Mode, CXL 68B and 256B Flit Modes (where CXL.io, CXL.cache, and CXL.mem are negotiated independently), and user-defined Streaming Protocols.
*   **Protocol Layer Compliance:** The text defines specific mandatory support requirements for interoperability, such as the necessity for a Protocol Layer to support PCIe non-Flit mode under certain parameters and the hierarchical requirements for supporting various CXL Flit modes.

structure.title: Protocol Layer
structure.summary:
This partial document is an excerpt from the Universal Chiplet Interconnect express (UCIe) Specification (Revision 1.1), specifically focusing on the **Protocol Layer**. The main points covered include:

*   **Protocol Mapping and Support:** The document defines how UCIe maps various protocols, including PCIe (both Flit and non-Flit modes), CXL (68B and 256B Flit modes), and generic Streaming Protocols. It notes that PCIe non-Flit mode is transported using the CXL.io 68B Flit Format.
*   **Separation of Concerns:** It emphasizes the distinction between Protocol-related features (Transaction Layer, Link Layer flow control, etc.) and Flit Formats/packetization, noting that transport mechanisms are not strictly tied to specific protocol features.
*   **Interoperability Requirements:** The text outlines specific requirements for Protocol Layer interoperability, such as mandatory support for certain modes based on advertised parameters (e.g., if CXL 256B Flit Mode is supported, PCIe Flit Mode and 68B Flit Mode must also be supported for CXL.io).
*   **Negotiation and Communication:** It describes the role of the Die-to-Die (D2D) Adapter in negotiating operational modes with remote link partners during Link Training and communicating these modes to the Protocol Layer via the Flit-aware Die-to-Die Interface (FDI).
*   **Operational Modes and Formats:** The document introduces the concept of different operating modes that allow for trade-offs in efficiency and bandwidth, and provides a color-coding convention for illustrating how bits are populated by either the Protocol Layer or the Adapter within Flit Formats.
*   **Specification Compliance:** A summary table (Table 2-1) details the mandatory and optional requirements for different specification versions (PCIe, CXL 2.0, and CXL 3.0) regarding their supported protocol modes.

structure.title: PCIe
structure.summary:
This partial document is an excerpt from the Universal Chiplet Interconnect Express (UCIe) Specification (Revision 1.1), specifically focusing on the Die-to-Die (D2D) Adapter's role in protocol negotiation and the detailed implementation of PCIe protocol modes.

The main points covered include:

*   **D2D Adapter and Protocol Negotiation:** The role of the D2D Adapter in negotiating protocols and operational modes with remote Link partners during Link Training. It explains how different modes utilize various Flit Formats to balance efficiency, bandwidth, and interoperability.
*   **Flit Format Mechanics:** An overview of how the Protocol Layer transmits data over the Flit-aware Die-to-Die Interface (FDI) and the use of color-coding conventions to distinguish between bits populated by the Protocol Layer versus the Adapter.
*   **PCIe Protocol Support:** A detailed breakdown of how UCIe supports PCIe, including both Flit Mode and non-Flit Mode (via CXL.io 68B Flit Formats). It includes a requirement table mapping specification versions (PCIe, CXL 2.0, CXL 3.0) to mandatory and optional protocol modes.
*   **Specific PCIe Operating Formats:**
    *   **Raw Format (Optional):** Intended for UCIe Retimers transporting PCIe protocol, where the Protocol Layer handles retry, CRC, and FEC.
    *   **Standard 256B End Header Flit Format (Mandatory for PCIe Flit Mode):** Designed for interoperability with standard PCIe vendors, noting that certain DLLPs are not used over UCIe.
    *   **68B Flit Format (Mandatory for PCIe/CXL):** Utilizes the CXL.io transport mechanism for non-Flit mode operations.
    *   **Standard 256B Start Header Flit Format (Optional):** An alternative format for PCIe Flit Mode supported if specific protocol capabilities are present.
*   **Implementation Recommendations:** Repeated guidance for developers to optimize area and power efficiency by removing unnecessary logic (such as 8b/10b or non-Flit Mode CRC/Retry) that is redundant due to the UCIe Adapter's reliable transport.

nodes.title: Raw Format
nodes.summary:
This partial document is an excerpt from the Universal Chiplet Interconnect Express (UCIe) Specification, Revision 1.1. It details various Flit (Flow Control Unit) formats used for data transport within the UCIe framework, specifically focusing on how different protocols (such as PCIe and CXL) interact with the UCIe adapter.

The main points covered include:

*   **Raw Format (Optional):** Designed for UCIe Retimers transporting PCIe protocol, particularly in scenarios involving off-package interconnects between different racks or chassis. It notes that error handling (Retry, CRC, FEC) is managed by the Protocol Layer.
*   **Standard 256B End Header Flit Format (Mandatory for PCIe Flit Mode):** Aimed at ensuring interoperability with vendors supporting standard PCIe Flit Formats. It specifies rules for Die-to-Die Adapter fields, DLLP usage, and recommends optimizing out non-Flit mode logic to improve power and area efficiency.
*   **68B Flit Format (Mandatory for PCIe or CXL protocols):** Utilizes a transport mechanism similar to CXL.io. It outlines specific framing rules, requirements for driving certain bytes with fixed values, and how to handle framing errors within the UCIe Adapter.
*   **Standard 256B Start Header Flit Format (Optional for PCIe Flit Mode):** Similar to the End Header format, this is used when specific PCIe protocol capabilities are supported, emphasizing the need for the Protocol Layer to follow FDI transfer rules and optimize for efficiency.

nodes.title: Standard 256B End Header Flit Format
nodes.summary:
This partial document is an excerpt from the Universal Chiplet Interconnect Express (UCIe) Specification (Revision 1.1). It details various Flit (Flow Control Unit) formats used for data transport within the UCIe framework, specifically focusing on how different protocols like PCIe and CXL are handled. The main points covered include:

*   **Raw Format (Optional):** Designed for UCIe Retimers transporting PCIe protocol, particularly for off-package interconnects between different racks or chassis. It notes that error handling (Retry, CRC, FEC) is managed by the Protocol Layer.
*   **Standard 256B End Header Flit Format (Mandatory for PCIe Flit Mode):** Aimed at ensuring interoperability with vendors supporting standard PCIe Flit Formats. It specifies rules for Die-to-Die Adapter fields, DLLP usage, and recommends optimizing out non-Flit mode logic to improve power and area efficiency.
*   **68B Flit Format (Mandatory for PCIe or CXL protocols):** Utilizes a transport mechanism similar to CXL.io. It outlines specific framing rules, CRC handling for non-Flit mode, and recommendations for error detection and implementation efficiency.
*   **Standard 256B Start Header Flit Format (Optional for PCIe Flit Mode):** Similar to the End Header format, this is used if the Standard Start Header for PCIe protocol capability is supported, following specific rules for FDI transfer and protocol layer optimization.

nodes.title: 68B Flit Format
nodes.summary:
This partial document is an excerpt from the Universal Chiplet Interconnect Express (UCIe) Specification, Revision 1.1. It details various Flit (Flow Control Unit) formats used for data transport within the UCIe framework, specifically focusing on how different protocols (such as PCIe and CXL) interact with the UCIe adapter.

The main points covered include:

*   **Raw Format (Optional):** Designed for UCIe Retimers transporting PCIe protocol, particularly in scenarios involving off-package interconnects (e.g., connecting a CPU and I/O device across different racks). It notes that error handling (Retry, CRC, FEC) is managed by the Protocol Layer.
*   **Standard 256B End Header Flit Format (Mandatory for PCIe Flit Mode):** Aimed at ensuring interoperability with vendors supporting standard PCIe Flit Formats. It specifies rules for Die-to-Die Adapter fields, DLLP usage, and recommends optimizing out non-Flit mode logic to improve power and area efficiency.
*   **68B Flit Format (Mandatory for PCIe or CXL protocols):** Utilizes a transport mechanism similar to CXL.io. It outlines specific framing rules, requirements for driving certain bytes with fixed values, and how to handle framing errors within the UCIe adapter's reliable transport environment.
*   **Standard 256B Start Header Flit Format (Optional for PCIe Flit Mode):** Similar to the End Header format, this is used when specific PCIe protocol capabilities are supported, emphasizing the need for the Protocol Layer to drive specific fields and optimize for efficiency.

nodes.title: Standard 256B Start Header Flit Format
nodes.summary:
This partial document is an excerpt from the Universal Chiplet Interconnect Express (UCIe) Specification, Revision 1.1, focusing on the various Flit (Flow Control Unit) formats used for transporting PCIe and CXL protocols.

The main points covered include:

*   **PCIe Flit Formats:** Detailed descriptions of several PCIe-related formats, including:
    *   **Raw Format (Optional):** Designed for UCIe Retimers transporting PCIe protocol across off-package interconnects.
    *   **Standard 256B End Header Flit Format (Mandatory):** Aimed at ensuring interoperability with standard PCIe Flit Mode vendors.
    *   **68B Flit Format (Mandatory):** Required when PCIe or CXL protocols are supported, utilizing transport mechanisms similar to CXL.io.
    *   **Standard 256B Start Header Flit Format (Optional):** An alternative for PCIe Flit Mode.
    *   **Latency-Optimized 256B with Optional Bytes Flit Format (Optional):** A specialized format for PCIe protocol capability.

*   **CXL 256B Flit Mode:** An overview of operational formats for CXL, including:
    *   **Raw Format (Optional):** Intended for UCIe Retimers transporting CXL 256B Flit Mode, specifically for off-package interconnect scenarios.
    *   **Latency-Optimized 256B Flit Formats:** Highly recommended formats that allow the Protocol Layer to consume Flits at 128B boundaries to significantly reduce accumulation latency. It notes two variations that offer different packing efficiencies for CXL.io and CXL.cachemem.

*   **Implementation Recommendations:** Throughout the text, the document provides guidance on optimizing designs for area and power efficiency (such as removing unnecessary CRC/Retry logic) and specifies how the Protocol Layer should handle reserved fields, DLLPs (Data Link Layer Packets), and error detection/characterization.

nodes.title: Latency-Optimized 256B with Optional Bytes Flit Format
nodes.summary:
This partial document is an excerpt from the Universal Chiplet Interconnect Express (UCIe) Specification (Revision 1.1), specifically focusing on Flit (Flow Control Unit) formats for PCIe and CXL protocols. The main points covered include:

*   **PCIe Flit Mode Optimization:** Details regarding the "Latency-Optimized 256B with Optional Bytes Flit Format," which is an optional format designed to support PCIe protocol capabilities. It outlines requirements for the Protocol Layer, such as driving specific reserved fields to zero and the recommendation to optimize out unnecessary CRC/Retry or framing logic to improve power and area efficiency.
*   **CXL 256B Flit Mode:** An overview of the operational formats available for CXL over UCIe, noting that certain bytes are inserted by the Adapter and must be managed by the Protocol Layer (driven as 0 on the transmitter and ignored on the receiver).
*   **Raw Format for CXL:** Describes an optional "Raw Format" intended for use with UCIe Retimers in off-package interconnect scenarios (e.g., connecting a CPU and I/O device across different racks). It specifies that the Protocol Layer is responsible for Retry, CRC, and FEC, and recommends that Retimers use parity or CRC to debug interconnect errors.
*   **Latency-Optimized CXL Flit Formats:** Discusses two recommended formats derived from the CXL Specification designed to reduce accumulation latency by allowing the Protocol Layer to consume Flits at 128B boundaries. It highlights how these formats improve packing efficiency for CXL.io and CXL.cachemem and notes that support for these formats is negotiated during link bring-up.
*   **Data Link Layer Packet (DLLP) Management:** Specifies that certain DLLPs (such as Ack, Nak, PM, and Link Management) are not used over UCIe for specific modes, while others follow standard CXL/PCIe rules.

structure.title: CXL 256B Flit Mode
structure.summary:
This partial document is an excerpt from the Universal Chiplet Interconnect Express (UCIe) Specification (Revision 1.1), specifically focusing on technical details regarding Flit (Flow Control Unit) formats for PCIe and CXL protocols. The main points covered include:

*   **PCIe Flit Mode Optimization:** It describes an optional "Latency-Optimized 256B with Optional Bytes" Flit format for PCIe. It outlines requirements for the Protocol Layer, such as driving zeros on reserved fields, the exclusion of certain Data Link Layer Packets (DLLPs) like PM and Link Management, and recommendations for optimizing area and power by removing unnecessary CRC/Retry logic.
*   **CXL 256B Flit Mode:** The document details various operational formats for CXL over UCIe, noting that the Adapter inserts specific bytes into the data path.
*   **Raw Format for CXL:** An optional format intended for use with UCIe Retimers in off-package interconnect scenarios (e.g., connecting a CPU to an I/O device across different racks). In this mode, the Protocol Layer handles Retry, CRC, and FEC, and the document provides recommendations for error checking to debug interconnects.
*   **Latency-Optimized CXL Flit Formats:** It discusses two recommended formats derived from the CXL Specification designed to reduce accumulation latency by allowing the Protocol Layer to consume Flits at 128B boundaries. It highlights how these formats improve packing efficiency for CXL.io and CXL.cachemem and notes that support for these formats is negotiated during link bring-up.

nodes.title: Raw Format
nodes.summary:

*   **PCIe Flit Mode Optimization:** Details regarding the "Latency-Optimized 256B with Optional Bytes Flit Format," which is an optional format designed to support PCIe protocol capabilities. It outlines requirements for the Protocol Layer, such as driving specific reserved fields to zero and the recommendation to optimize out unnecessary CRC/Retry or framing logic to improve power and area efficiency.
*   **CXL 256B Flit Mode:** An overview of the operational formats for CXL over UCIe, noting that the Adapter inserts specific bytes into the data path.
*   **Raw Format for CXL:** Describes an optional "Raw Format" intended for use with UCIe Retimers in off-package interconnect scenarios (e.g., connecting a CPU and I/O device across different racks). It specifies that the Protocol Layer is responsible for Retry, CRC, and FEC, and recommends that Retimers use parity or CRC to debug interconnect errors.
*   **Latency-Optimized CXL Flit Formats:** Discusses two recommended formats derived from the CXL Specification designed to reduce accumulation latency by allowing the Protocol Layer to consume Flits at 128B boundaries. It highlights how these formats improve packing efficiency (providing extra bytes for CXL.io TLP information or CXL.cachemem H-slots) and notes that support is negotiated during link bring-up.
*   **Protocol Layer Responsibilities:** Across both PCIe and CXL sections, the text defines how Data Link Layer Packets (DLLPs) are handled, the necessity of driving specific bytes for Flit_Marker assignment, and the exclusion of certain DLLPs (like PM and Link Management) over UCIe.

nodes.title: Latency-Optimized 256B Flit Formats
nodes.summary:
This partial document is an excerpt from the Universal Chiplet Interconnect Express (UCIe) Specification (Revision 1.1, Version 1.0), specifically focusing on the technical details of Flit (Flow Control Unit) formats for CXL (Compute Express Link) protocols.

The main points covered include:

*   **PCIe Flit Mode:** Describes the "Latency-Optimized 256B with Optional Bytes" format, noting its optional nature and requirements for the Protocol Layer to optimize for area and power efficiency by removing unnecessary CRC/Retry logic.
*   **CXL 256B Flit Mode:** Details four operational formats for CXL 256B Flit Mode:
    *   **Raw Format (Optional):** Designed for UCIe Retimers in off-package interconnect scenarios, where the Protocol Layer handles all byte population, including Retry and CRC.
    *   **Latency-Optimized 256B Flit Formats (Recommended):** Two formats derived from the CXL Specification that allow the Protocol Layer to consume Flits at 128B boundaries to reduce latency. One format offers higher packing efficiency by providing extra bytes for CXL.io or CXL.cachemem.
    *   **Standard 256B Start Header Flit Format (Mandatory):** Ensures interoperability with vendors supporting standard CXL 256B Flit formats.
*   **CXL 68B Flit Mode:** Outlines two operational formats:
    *   **Raw Format (Optional):** Similar to the 256B version, intended for use with UCIe Retimers.
    *   **68B Flit Format (Mandatory):** Designed for interoperability with baseline CXL formats. It specifies how the Die-to-Die Adapter inserts headers and CRC, and notes that for CXL.io, Retry is handled by the Adapter rather than the Protocol Layer.
*   **Protocol Layer Requirements:** Across all modes, the document specifies how the Protocol Layer must interact with the Die-to-Die Adapter, including driving specific fields to zero, handling DLLPs (Data Link Layer Packets), and managing error containment (such as Viral containment for CXL.cachemem).

nodes.title: Standard 256B Start Header Flit Format
nodes.summary:
This partial document is an excerpt from the Universal Chiplet Interconnect Express (UCIe) Specification (Revision 1.1), specifically focusing on the implementation and formatting of CXL (Compute Express Link) protocols over the UCIe interface. The main points covered include:

*   **Standard 256B Start Header Flit Format:** Details the mandatory requirements for supporting the "CXL 256B Flit Mode" to ensure interoperability with other vendors. It outlines how the Protocol Layer must handle reserved fields, the exclusion of certain Data Link Layer Packets (DLLPs) for CXL.io, and the use of the `lp_corrupt_crc` signal in CXL.cachemem to optimize latency while maintaining "Viral containment."
*   **CXL 68B Flit Mode:** Describes two distinct operational formats for the 68B Flit Mode:
    *   **Raw Format (Optional):** Intended for use with UCIe Retimers in scenarios where a CPU and I/O device are in different racks/chassis. In this mode, the Protocol Layer is responsible for populating all bytes and managing Retry and CRC.
    *   **68B Flit Format (Mandatory):** Designed for interoperability with baseline CXL formats. It specifies how the Protocol Layer presents 64B of the Flit to the Die-to-Die Adapter, which then inserts the necessary header and CRC. It also notes specific rules for CXL.io, such as the handling of DLLPs, the requirement for the Adapter (rather than the Protocol Layer) to handle Retry, and instructions for driving/ignoring specific CRC and sequence number fields.

structure.title: CXL 68B Flit Mode
structure.summary:
This partial document is an excerpt from the Universal Chiplet Interconnect Express (UCIe) Specification (Revision 1.1), specifically focusing on the implementation and formatting of CXL (Compute Express Link) protocols over the UCIe interface. The main points covered include:

*   **Standard 256B Start Header Flit Format:** Details the mandatory requirements for interoperability with vendors supporting the CXL 256B Flit Mode. It outlines how the Protocol Layer must handle reserved fields, the exclusion of certain Data Link Layer Packets (DLLPs) for CXL.io, and the use of the `lp_corrupt_crc` signal in CXL.cachemem to optimize latency while ensuring "Viral containment."
*   **CXL 68B Flit Mode:** Describes the two operational formats available for this mode:

    *   **68B Flit Format (Mandatory):** Designed for interoperability with baseline CXL formats. It explains the division of labor between the Protocol Layer (which provides 64B of the Flit) and the Die-to-Die Adapter (which inserts the header and CRC).
*   **Protocol Layer and Adapter Responsibilities:** The text specifies how different layers manage error handling (Retry, CRC, and LCRC), DLLP usage (such as the exclusion of Ack/Nak for certain CXL.io modes), and framing rules to ensure consistent operation across the UCIe interface.

*   **Standard 256B Start Header Flit Format:** Details the mandatory requirements for interoperability with vendors supporting the CXL 256B Flit Mode. It outlines how the Protocol Layer must manage specific fields, the exclusion of certain Data Link Layer Packets (DLLPs) for CXL.io, and the use of the `lp_corrupt_crc` signal in CXL.cachemem to optimize latency while ensuring "Viral containment."
*   **CXL 68B Flit Mode:** Describes the two operational formats available for this mode:
    *   **Raw Format (Optional):** Intended for use with UCIe Retimers in scenarios where a CPU and I/O device are in different racks/chassis. In this mode, the Protocol Layer is responsible for Retry and CRC.
    *   **68B Flit Format (Mandatory):** Designed for interoperability with baseline CXL formats. It explains the division of labor between the Protocol Layer (which provides 64B of the Flit) and the Die-to-Die Adapter (which inserts the header and CRC).
*   **Protocol Management and Error Handling:** Specifies how DLLPs (such as Ack, Nak, and PM) are handled differently depending on the mode, the rules for driving specific bytes (like LCRC) to maintain framing consistency, and how reliability and retry mechanisms are distributed between the Protocol Layer and the Adapter.

nodes.title: 68B Flit Format
nodes.summary:
This partial document from the Universal Chiplet Interconnect Express (UCIe) Specification (Revision 1.1) details the various Flit (Flow Control Unit) formats and protocol modes supported by the interface, specifically focusing on CXL and Streaming protocols.

The main points covered include:

*   **CXL 256B Flit Mode:** Describes the mandatory Standard 256B Start Header Flit Format designed for interoperability with vendors supporting standard CXL specifications. It outlines how the Protocol Layer interacts with the Die-to-Die Adapter and mentions specific mechanisms like the `lp_corrupt_crc` signal for latency optimization and viral containment in CXL.cachemem.
*   **CXL 68B Flit Mode:** Details two operational formats:
    *   **Raw Format (Optional):** Used primarily for UCIe Retimers where the Protocol Layer populates all bytes and handles Retry and CRC.
    *   **68B Flit Format (Mandatory):** Designed for interoperability with baseline CXL formats. It explains the division of labor between the Protocol Layer (providing 64B) and the Die-to-Die Adapter (inserting headers and CRC). It also specifies rules for CXL.io and CXL.cachemem, including how retry and error handling are managed.
*   **Streaming Protocol:** Defines the default protocol used when PCIe or CXL are not negotiated. It covers several formats:
    *   **Raw Format:** A mandatory format for Adapter implementations where the Protocol Layer populates all bytes.
    *   **68B Flit Format:** An optional format for streaming protocols that utilizes the Adapter to insert headers and CRC.
    *   **Standard 256B Flit Formats:** Optional formats (Start Header or End Header) that allow implementations to utilize standard 256B structures for streaming.

structure.title: Streaming protocol
structure.summary:
This partial document is an excerpt from the Universal Chiplet Interconnect Express (UCIe) Specification, Revision 1.1. It focuses on technical details regarding error handling, link initialization, and specifically the "Streaming protocol" (Section 2.4). 

The main points covered include:
* **Error Handling and Link Initialization:** Guidance on treating transport and framing errors as uncorrectable internal errors, specific handling for CXL.cachemem (regarding the "Ak" field and Retry Flits), and the process for initiating links via the INIT.Param Flit.
* **Streaming Protocol Overview:** Definition of the Streaming protocol as the default fallback when PCIe or CXL protocols are not negotiated, and the criteria for supporting "Streaming Flit Format" capabilities via the UCIe Link Capability register.
* **Flit Format Specifications:** Detailed descriptions of different data formats used within the Streaming protocol:
    * **Raw Format:** A mandatory format for Adapter implementations where interoperability is vendor-defined.
    * **68B Flit Format:** An optional format where the Die-to-Die Adapter manages the insertion and stripping of 2B Flit Headers and 2B CRC bytes to present 64B per Flit to the Protocol Layer.
    * **Standard 256B Flit Formats:** Options for using Start Header or End Header formats, detailing how the Protocol Layer and Adapter interact with reserved bits and CRC bytes during data transmission and reception.

nodes.title: Raw Format
nodes.summary:
This partial document is an excerpt from the Universal Chiplet Interconnect Express (UCIe) Specification, Revision 1.1. It focuses on technical details regarding error handling, CXL protocol specifics, and the various formats available under the Streaming Protocol. Key points include:

*   **Error Handling and CXL Specifics:** Guidance on treating transport and framing errors as uncorrectable internal errors, and specific implementation rules for CXL.cachemem, such as reserved fields and link initialization procedures.
*   **Streaming Protocol Overview:** Definition of the Streaming protocol as the default option when PCIe or CXL are not negotiated, including the conditions under which "Streaming Flit Format" capability is supported.
*   **Flit Formats:** Detailed descriptions of different data transfer formats:
    *   **Raw Format:** A mandatory format for Streaming protocol support where interoperability is vendor-defined.
    *   **68B Flit Format:** An optional format where the Die-to-Die Adapter manages headers, CRC, and byte shifting to present 64B per Flit to the Protocol Layer.
    *   **Standard 256B Flit Formats:** Options including Start Header and End Header formats, detailing how the Protocol Layer and Adapter interact with reserved bits and CRC during data transmission and reception.

nodes.title: 68B Flit Format
nodes.summary:
This partial document is an excerpt from the Universal Chiplet Interconnect Express (UCIe) Specification, Revision 1.1. It focuses on technical details regarding error handling, link initialization for CXL.cachemem, and the specifics of the Streaming protocol. Key points covered include:

*   **Error Handling and CXL.cachemem:** Guidance on treating transport and framing errors as uncorrectable internal errors, and specific protocols for CXL.cachemem, such as reserved fields and the handling of Retry Flits and Link Initialization.
*   **Streaming Protocol Overview:** Definition of the Streaming protocol as the default when PCIe or CXL are not negotiated, including the conditions under which Streaming Flit Format capability is supported.
*   **Flit Formats:** Detailed descriptions of various data formats used within the Streaming protocol:
    *   **Raw Format:** A mandatory format for Adapter implementations where interoperability is vendor-defined.
    *   **68B Flit Format:** An optional format where the Die-to-Die Adapter manages headers, CRC, and byte shifting to present 64B per Flit to the Protocol Layer.
    *   **Standard 256B Flit Formats:** Options for using Start Header or End Header Flit formats, detailing how the Protocol Layer and Adapter interact with reserved bits and CRC bytes.

nodes.title: Standard 256B Flit Formats
nodes.summary:
This partial document is an excerpt from the Universal Chiplet Interconnect Express (UCIe) Specification, Revision 1.1. It focuses on the technical details of the Streaming protocol and its various Flit (Flow Control Unit) formats.

The main points covered include:

*   **Error Handling and CXL Specifics:** Brief mentions of how transport and framing errors are treated, specific reserved fields and retry mechanisms for CXL.cachemem, and requirements for viral containment in 68B Flit Mode.
*   **Streaming Protocol Overview:** Definition of the Streaming protocol as the default option when PCIe or CXL protocols are not negotiated, and the criteria for supporting "Streaming Flit Format" capabilities via the UCIe Link Capability register.
*   **Flit Format Specifications:** Detailed descriptions of the different operational formats available under the Streaming protocol:
    *   **Raw Format:** A mandatory format for Adapter implementations where interoperability is vendor-defined.
    *   **68B Flit Format:** An optional format where the Die-to-Die Adapter manages Flit Headers, CRC, and byte shifting to present 64B per Flit to the Protocol Layer.
    *   **Standard 256B Flit Formats:** Options including Start Header and End Header formats, detailing how the Protocol Layer and Adapter interact regarding reserved bits and CRC.
    *   **Latency-Optimized 256B Flit Formats:** Options that include or exclude optional bytes, following similar interaction rules between the Protocol Layer and the Adapter as the Standard 256B formats.

nodes.title: Latency-Optimized 256B Flit Formats
nodes.summary:
This partial document is an excerpt from the Universal Chiplet Interconnect express (UCIe) Specification, Revision 1.1. It covers two primary technical areas:

*   **Latency-Optimized 256B Flit Formats:** It describes specific flit formats used when Streaming Flit Format capability is supported. It outlines the interaction between the Protocol Layer and the Adapter, noting that the Protocol Layer provides 256B per flit via the Flit-aware Die-to-Die interface (FDI), while the Adapter is responsible for filling in the Flit Header and CRC bytes.
*   **Die-to-Die (D2D) Adapter:** It defines the core responsibilities of the D2D Adapter, which include reliable data transfer (CRC/Retry/Parity), arbitration and muxing for multiple protocol layers, link state management, and protocol/parameter negotiation. It also details the Adapter's interfaces, specifically the FDI (connecting to the Protocol Layer) and the RDI (connecting to the Physical Layer), and mentions the ability to multiplex multiple protocol stacks onto a single physical link.

structure.title: Die-to-Die Adapter
structure.summary:
This document excerpt from the Universal Chiplet Interconnect Express (UCIe) Specification (Revision 1.1) details the functionalities and operational rules of the **Die-to-Die (D2D) Adapter**. 

The main points covered are:

*   **D2D Adapter Responsibilities:** The Adapter manages reliable data transfer (via CRC/Retry or parity), arbitration and muxing for multiple protocol layers, link state management, and protocol/parameter negotiation with remote partners.
*   **Interfaces:** It defines the Adapter's connection points, specifically the Flit-aware Die-to-Die interface (FDI) used to communicate with the Protocol Layer and the raw Die-to-Die interface (RDI) used to communicate with the Physical Layer.
*   **Stack Multiplexing:** The document explains how multiple protocol stacks can be multiplexed onto a single physical link to optimize cost and efficiency. It distinguishes between two modes:
    *   **Multi_Protocol_Enable:** Supports two stacks of the same protocol and capabilities, where each stack uses half the available bandwidth. It outlines strict rules for preventing consecutive flits from the same stack by using NOP (No-Operation) flits.
    *   **Enhanced Multi_Protocol_Enable:** Supports dynamic multiplexing between stacks of the same or different protocols. This mode allows for bandwidth throttling (between 50% and 100%) and requires the Adapter to support round-robin arbitration per flit.
*   **Implementation Logic:** It provides technical requirements for flit handling, retry buffers, stack identifiers for de-multiplexing, and the management of independent link state machines for each protocol stack.

structure.title: Stack Multiplexing
structure.summary:
This partial document is an excerpt from the Universal Chiplet Interconnect Express (UCIe) Specification (Revision 1.1), specifically focusing on the mechanisms for Stack Multiplexing and the stages of Link Initialization.

The main points covered are:

*   **Stack Multiplexing:** The document details two methods for multiplexing protocol stacks on a single physical link:
    *   **Multi_Protocol_Enable:** Supports two multiplexed stacks of the same protocol and capabilities, each utilizing half the available bandwidth. It outlines strict requirements for the Adapter to prevent consecutive flits from the same stack by using NOP (No-Operation) flits.
    *   **Enhanced Multi_Protocol_Enable:** Supports dynamic multiplexing between two stacks of the same or different protocols. This feature allows for flexible bandwidth allocation (up to 100% for a single layer) and requires the use of round-robin arbitration per flit.
    *   **Implementation Details:** It describes the use of stack identifiers in flit headers for de-multiplexing, the maintenance of independent Link state machines, and the role of NOP flits in managing data streams.

*   **Link Initialization:** The document outlines the four-stage process required before protocol flit transfer can begin on the mainband:
    *   **Stage 0:** Die-specific reset flows that occur independently for each die.
    *   **Stage 1:** Sideband initialization.
    *   **Stage 2:** Mainband training and repair.
    *   **Stage 3 (Adapter Initialization):** Parameter exchanges between Adapters to negotiate protocols and flit formats.

*   **Adapter Initialization (Stage 3):** A detailed look at the transition from the Active State (following Stage 2) to the completion of Stage 3, specifically focusing on determining local capabilities, such as Physical Layer training results and the ability to support Retry mechanisms.

structure.title: Link Initialization
structure.summary:
This partial document is an excerpt from the Universal Chiplet Interconnect Express (UCIe) Specification (Revision 1.1). It focuses on the technical details of the **Link Initialization** process, which is required before protocol Flit transfer can begin on the mainband.

The main points covered include:
*   **The Four Stages of Link Initialization:** An overview of the progression from Stage 0 (die-specific reset flows) through Stage 1 (sideband initialization), Stage 2 (mainband training and repair), and Stage 3 (parameter exchanges between Adapters).
*   **Stage 3 (Adapter Initialization):** A detailed look at the transition from the "Reset" to the "Active" state once Stage 2 is complete.
*   **Local Capability Determination:** The requirement for the Adapter to assess Physical Layer training results, determine if a "Retry" is necessary based on link speed and configuration, and advertise these capabilities to the remote link partner.
*   **Workflow Visualization:** Reference to a high-level flow (Figure 3-3) illustrating the time progression and dependencies between Die 0 and Die 1 during the initialization stages.

nodes.title: Stage 3 of Link Initialization: Adapter Initialization
nodes.summary:
This partial document is an excerpt from the Universal Chiplet Interconnect Express (UCIe) Specification, Revision 1.1. It focuses on the technical details of the **Link Initialization** process, which is required before protocol Flit transfer can begin on the mainband.

The main points covered include:
*   **The Four Stages of Link Initialization:** An overview of the sequential flow, including Stage 0 (die-specific reset), Stage 1 (sideband initialization), Stage 2 (mainband training and repair), and Stage 3 (parameter exchanges between Adapters).
*   **Adapter Initialization (Stage 3):** A detailed look at the transition from the "Reset" to the "Active" state via the RDI state machine.
*   **Determining Local Capabilities:** The requirement for the Adapter to assess Physical Layer training results, determine if a "Retry" is necessary based on link speed and configuration, and advertise these capabilities to the remote link partner.

nodes.title: Part 1: Determine Local Capabilities
nodes.summary:
This partial document is an excerpt from the Universal Chiplet Interconnect Express (UCIe) Specification, Revision 1.1, focusing on the technical details of the **Link Initialization** process. 

The main points covered include:

*   **Stages of Link Initialization:** An overview of the four-stage process required before protocol Flit transfer can begin on the mainband:
    *   **Stage 0:** Die-specific reset flow.
    *   **Stage 1:** Sideband initialization.
    *   **Stage 2:** Mainband training and lane repair.
    *   **Stage 3:** Adapter initialization and parameter exchange.
*   **Adapter Initialization (Stage 3):** Detailed steps for the Adapter to transition from the Reset state to the Active state, specifically focusing on determining local capabilities and performing parameter exchanges with a remote link partner.
*   **Local Capability Determination:** The requirement for the Adapter to assess Physical Layer training results, determine if a "Retry" is necessary, and identify Retimer-specific capabilities (such as Receiver Buffer credits).
*   **Parameter Exchange and Negotiation:** A detailed breakdown of the specific capabilities that must be negotiated between link partners via sideband messages. This includes support for various protocols and formats, such as:
    *   **Protocol Modes:** Raw Format, Streaming, PCIe Flit Mode, and CXL Flit Modes (68B and 256B).
    *   **Multi-Protocol Support:** Capabilities for operating with multiple FDI instances (Stack 0 and Stack 1).
    *   **Specialized Formats:** CXL Latency Optimized formats (Format 5 and Format 6).
    *   **Hardware-Specific Parameters:** Retimer identification and Retimer credit advertising.

nodes.title: Part 2: Parameter Exchange with remote Link partner
nodes.summary:
This partial document is an excerpt from the Universal Chiplet Interconnect Express (UCIe) Specification (Revision 1.1), specifically focusing on the technical details of Link Initialization and Parameter Exchange. The main points covered include:

*   **Parameter Exchange and Capability Negotiation:** Detailed descriptions of the capabilities that must be negotiated between Link partners (such as Raw Format, various Flit Modes for PCIe and CXL, Streaming protocols, and Retry support) using sideband messages ({AdvCap.Adapter} and {FinCap.Adapter}).
*   **Protocol and Flit Mode Determination:** The logic and rules used to finalize configurations, including how "Upstream" and "Downstream" ports interact, how multi-protocol capabilities (Stack 0 and Stack 1) are enabled, and how the "lowest common denominator" is used when capabilities do not match.
*   **Multi-Protocol Support:** Procedures for negotiating "Enhanced Multi-Protocol" and "Multi-Protocol" modes, including the use of specific sideband messages to manage different protocol stacks on a single adapter.
*   **Retimer Functionality:** Specific requirements for UCIe Retimers, including the advertisement of Retimer credits and the identification of Upstream/Downstream ports.
*   **Error Handling and Timeouts:** The requirement for Adapters to implement an 8ms timeout for successful Parameter Exchange completion and the handling of "Stall" messages.
*   **FDI Bring-up and Operation Formats:** An overview of the transition to the FDI (Fabric Device Interface) bring-up stage and a description of different data operation formats, such as Raw Format (Format 1) and 68B Flit Format (Format 2).

nodes.title: Part 3: FDI bring up
nodes.summary:
This partial document is an excerpt from the Universal Chiplet Interconnect Express (UCIe) Specification, Revision 1.1. It details technical procedures regarding link initialization, data transfer formats, and interface specifications. The main points covered include:

*   **Link Initialization and Timeout Management:** Detailed rules for measuring timeouts during Parameter Exchange and Link State Machine transitions, including the handling of "stall" messages and the responsibilities of UCIe Retimers to prevent timeouts during capability resolution.
*   **FDI Bring-up (Part 3):** The process of transitioning the Flexible Die Interface (FDI) to an active state following successful parameter exchanges, allowing for protocol Flit transfer.
*   **Interface Data Widths:** An explanation of how data width is determined for the FDI (based on frequency and bandwidth) versus the RDI (which has a fixed minimum width per physical lane).
*   **Operation Formats:**
    *   **Raw Format (Format 1):** Used when Retry support is not required, allowing data to pass from the Protocol Layer to the Physical Layer without modification.
    *   **68B Flit Format (Format 2):** A format used for specific CXL and PCIe protocols, involving a 64B protocol payload supplemented by a 2-byte Flit Header and a 2-byte CRC suffix.

structure.title: Operation Formats
structure.summary:
This partial document is an excerpt from the Universal Chiplet Interconnect Express (UCIe) Specification, Revision 1.1. It details technical procedures regarding link initialization, data transfer formats, and interface management. The main points covered include:

*   **Link Initialization and Timeout Management:** Detailed rules for measuring timeouts during Parameter Exchange and Link State Machine transitions, including the handling of "stall" messages and the requirement for UCIe Retimers to resolve capability advertisements.
*   **FDI Bring-up (Part 3):** The process of transitioning the Fabric Device Interface (FDI) to an active state following successful parameter exchanges, and how data width on the FDI is determined by frequency and bandwidth.
*   **Interface Data Widths:** A distinction between the variable data width of the FDI (based on frequency and lanes) and the fixed minimum data width of the Reference Data Interface (RDI).
*   **Operation Formats:** An overview of data transmission formats, specifically:
    *   **Raw Format (Format 1):** Used when Retry support is not required, allowing direct transfer from the Protocol Layer to the Physical Layer.
    *   **68B Flit Format (Format 2):** A format used for CXL and PCIe protocols involving a 64B protocol payload supplemented by a 2-byte Flit Header and a 2-byte CRC suffix.

nodes.title: Raw Format for all protocols
nodes.summary:
This partial document is an excerpt from the Universal Chiplet Interconnect Express (UCIe) Specification, Revision 1.1. It details technical procedures regarding link initialization, data interface management, and operational data formats. The main points covered include:

*   **Link Initialization and Timeout Management:** Specifications for measuring timeouts during Parameter Exchange and Link State Machine transitions, including the handling of "stall" messages and the responsibilities of UCIe Retimers to resolve capability advertisements.
*   **FDI Bring-up (Part 3):** The process of transitioning the Fabric Device Interface (FDI) to an active state following successful parameter exchanges, and how multiple stacks on a single Adapter manage this flow.
*   **Data Interface Widths:** The relationship between FDI data width, operating frequency, and total bandwidth, contrasted with the fixed data width requirements of the Refined Device Interface (RDI).
*   **Operation Formats:** An overview of data transmission formats, specifically:
    *   **Raw Format (Format 1):** Used when Retry support is not required, allowing direct transfer from the Protocol Layer to the Physical Layer.
    *   **68B Flit Format (Format 2):** A mandatory format for certain CXL and PCIe protocols, involving the addition of a 2-byte Flit Header and a 2-byte CRC suffix to 64B of protocol information.

nodes.title: 68B Flit Format
nodes.summary:
This partial document from the Universal Chiplet Interconnect Express (UCIe) Specification (Revision 1.1) details technical protocols regarding link initialization, interface data widths, and various Flit (Flow Control Unit) operation formats.

The main points covered include:

*   **Link Initialization and Timing:** Detailed procedures for Stage 3 of Link Initialization, specifically regarding Parameter Exchange completion, timeout management (8ms), and the role of UCIe Retimers in resolving capability advertisements to prevent timeouts.
*   **FDI (Fabric Data Interface) Bring-up:** The process of transitioning the Adapter to an active state following successful Parameter Exchanges, and the relationship between FDI data width, operating frequency, and total bandwidth.
*   **RDI (Raw Die-to-Die Interface) Specifications:** The fixed data width requirements for RDI and how it maps to physical lanes.
*   **Operation Formats:**
    *   **Raw Format (Format 1):** Used when Retry support is not required, allowing direct transfer from the Protocol Layer to the Physical Layer without modification.
    *   **68B Flit Format (Format 2):** A mandatory format for CXL 68B Flit Mode and PCIe Non-Flit Mode. It includes details on Flit Headers (with and without Retry), CRC computation, and the use of Pause of Data Stream (PDS) tokens to manage data termination and alignment.
    *   **Standard 256B Flit Formats (Formats 3 & 4):** Based on PCIe and CXL specifications, these formats cover "Start Header" and "End Header" configurations, including the handling of Data Link Layer Packets (DLLPs) and credit updates.
*   **Error Handling and Data Integrity:** The implementation of CRC (Cyclic Redundancy Check) across different formats and the mechanisms for handling Retry, Retrain, and PDS scenarios to ensure data alignment and error detection.

nodes.title: Standard 256B Flit Formats
nodes.summary:
This partial document is an excerpt from the Universal Chiplet Interconnect Express (UCIe) Specification (Revision 1.1), specifically focusing on the technical details of **256B Flit Formats**. The main points covered include:

*   **Standard 256B Flit Formats:** The document defines "Format 3" (Standard 256B End Header Flit Format) and "Format 4" (Standard 256B Start Header Flit Format), which are derived from PCIe and CXL specifications. It outlines the mandatory support requirements for PCIe and CXL Flit Modes and the optional support for Streaming protocols.
*   **Protocol Layer and Adapter Responsibilities:** It details how data is partitioned between the Protocol Layer and the Adapter. This includes how the Protocol Layer populates specific bits (such as Protocol Identifiers and Flit Markers) and how the Adapter manages Data Link Layer Packets (DLLPs), Flit Headers, and credit update information via the Flit Device Interface (FDI).
*   **Error Detection (CRC):** The text describes the computation of two sets of Cyclic Redundancy Checks (CRC0 and CRC1) used for error detection, specifying the byte ranges and message inputs used for different formats.
*   **Latency-Optimized Flit Formats:** The document introduces "Format 5" and "Format 6," which are designed to optimize latency. It distinguishes between these formats based on whether the Protocol Layer populates optional bytes.
*   **Flit Header Structures:** Through detailed tables (Table 3-4 and Table 3-5) and figure descriptions, the document specifies the bit-level organization of Flit Headers for various protocols (PCIe, CXL, and Streaming). This includes definitions for Protocol Identifiers, Stack Identifiers, Sequence Numbers, and Ack/Nak information, both for scenarios with and without Retry requirements.

nodes.title: Latency-Optimized 256B Flit Formats
nodes.summary:
This partial document from the Universal Chiplet Interconnect Express (UCIe) Specification (Revision 1.1) details the technical specifications for various Flit (Flow Control Unit) formats, with a specific focus on latency-optimized 256B Flit formats.

The main points covered include:

*   **Latency-Optimized 256B Flit Formats:** The document defines two specific formats: **Format 5** (without optional bytes) and **Format 6** (with optional bytes). It notes that Format 6 is recommended for CXL 256B Flit Mode to achieve maximum latency benefits.
*   **Protocol Layer Interactions:** It explains the distinction between the two formats based on whether the Protocol Layer populates optional bytes. It also outlines specific requirements for Streaming, PCIe, and CXL protocols, including restrictions on Flit Header bit encodings.
*   **Flit Header Structure:** A detailed breakdown of the Flit Header (Byte 0 and Byte 1) is provided, including the Protocol Identifier, Stack Identifier, Sequence Numbering, and Ack/Nak information for different protocol modes (CXL.io, CXL.cachemem, PCIe, and Streaming).
*   **Error Detection (CRC):** The document describes the computation of two sets of Cyclic Redundancy Checks (CRC0 and CRC1) applied to specific byte ranges within the 256B Flit to ensure data integrity.
*   **Visual Data Mapping:** Through several figures (3-20 to 3-24), the document provides architectural diagrams showing the byte-level layout of different Flit formats, including the placement of Flit Chunks, Data Link Protocol (DLP) bytes, Flit Markers, and CRC bytes.
*   **Summary of All Flit Formats:** A comprehensive summary table (Table 3-6) compares six different Flit formats (Format 1 through Format 6), ranging from "Raw" to "Latency-Optimized," detailing the responsibilities of the Protocol Layer and the Adapter for each.

nodes.title: Flit Format-related Implementation Requirements for Protocol Layer and Adapter
nodes.summary:
This partial document is an excerpt from the Universal Chiplet Interconnect Express (UCIe) Specification, Revision 1.1. It focuses on the technical requirements and negotiation processes for different Flit (Flow Control Unit) formats used within the Protocol and Adapter layers.

The main points covered include:

*   **Summary of Flit Formats:** A detailed breakdown of six distinct Flit formats (ranging from "Raw" to "Latency-Optimized 256B with Optional Bytes"). The document describes the responsibilities of the Protocol Layer and the Adapter for each format, specifically regarding how data is transmitted on the FDI (Fabric Device Interface) and RDI (Raw Die Interface), including the handling of headers, CRC (Cyclic Redundancy Check), and byte shifting.
*   **Protocol Mapping and Implementation Requirements:** A mapping that defines which Flit formats are mandatory, optional, or strongly recommended for specific protocols, including PCIe (Non-Flit and Flit modes), CXL (68B and 256B Flit modes), and Streaming protocols.
*   **Protocol and Flit Format Negotiation:** An explanation of the decision-making process used during the initial link bring-up to determine the operating protocol and Flit format. It notes that once negotiated, these settings remain fixed until the Physical Layer enters a Reset state.
*   **Decision Truth Tables:** The inclusion of logic tables (Truth Tables) used to automate the selection of:
    *   The operating protocol (PCIe, CXL, or Streaming) based on capability bits.
    *   The specific Flit format for PCIe and CXL protocols.
    *   The Flit format for Streaming protocols based on the logical AND of advertised capabilities between link sides.

structure.title: Decision table for Flit Format and Protocol
structure.summary:
This partial document is an excerpt from the Universal Chiplet Interconnect Express (UCIe) Specification, Revision 1.1. It focuses on the technical mechanisms for protocol negotiation, flit format determination, and the hierarchical structure of link state management.

The main points covered are:

*   **Protocol and Flit Format Negotiation:** The document details how the UCIe link determines the operating protocol (such as PCIe, CXL, or Streaming) and the corresponding Flit Format during the initial link bring-up. It emphasizes that once these are negotiated, they remain fixed until the Physical Layer enters a Reset state.
*   **Decision Tables (Truth Tables):** The text provides three specific truth tables used to automate the selection process:
    *   **Table 3-8:** Determines the Protocol based on capability bits (e.g., CXL, PCIe, or Streaming).
    *   **Table 3-9:** Determines the Flit Format for PCIe or CXL protocols when specific enhanced or latency-optimized capabilities are not negotiated.
    *   **Table 3-10:** Determines the Flit Format for Streaming Protocols based on the logical AND of advertised capabilities between link partners.
*   **Flit Format Prioritization:** It explains the priority logic for different flit formats, noting that "Raw Format" has high priority for interoperability, while "Latency-Optimized" formats are prioritized for performance.
*   **State Machine Hierarchy:** The document describes a hierarchical approach to managing link states across different layers to ensure well-defined functionality. This includes:
    *   **vLSM (Virtual Link State Machines):** Used for CXL configurations to coordinate transitions via ALMPs.
    *   **Adapter LSM (Link State Machine):** Coordinates link states with remote partners using sideband messages.
    *   **RDI SM (RDI State Machine):** Abstracts Physical Layer states for upper layers.
*   **State Transition Rules:** It outlines the required sequencing for state transitions, specifically noting that for "Active" states, the RDI SM must be active before the Adapter LSM, which in turn must be active before the vLSMs. Conversely, "Retrain" states must propagate from the RDI SM down to the Adapter LSMs.

structure.title: State Machine Hierarchy
structure.summary:
This partial document from the Universal Chiplet Interconnect Express (UCIe) Specification (Revision 1.1) details the mechanisms for link state management and power management within the UCIe architecture. The main points covered include:

*   **State Machine Hierarchy:** The document describes a hierarchical approach to managing link states to ensure well-defined functionality across different layers. It outlines the roles of various state machines, including the vLSM (virtual Link State Machine), the Adapter LSM (Link State Machine), and the RDI (Raw Die-to-Die Interface) SM. It provides specific examples of these hierarchies for CXL configurations versus PCIe or Streaming protocols.
*   **State Transition Rules:** It establishes strict sequencing rules for transitioning between different states, such as Active, Retrain, Power Management (PM), LinkError, and LinkReset/Disabled. These rules define the required order of operations between the RDI, Adapter, and vLSM layers to ensure synchronization between local and remote link partners.
*   **Retimer Responsibilities:** The text specifies the role of UCIe Retimers in negotiating state transitions with remote partners to maintain synchronization and prevent timeouts, including requirements for handling low-power states and "Stall" encoding.
*   **Power Management (PM) Link States:** The document outlines mandatory power management states (L1 and L2) for PCIe and CXL protocols. It explains how these states enable optimizations like global clock gating and system-level flows (e.g., C-states).
*   **PM Entry Sequences:** It describes the process for entering power management states, noting that while CXL follows specific handshake rules defined in the CXL Specification, PCIe in UCIe Flit Mode relies on Protocol Layer requests based on link idle time rather than DLLP handshakes.
